narrative: Your loan application for 1108 Deutsche Marks to cover repairs was carefully reviewed by our credit model, which looks at patterns from past borrowers in 1970s Germany to assess risk. The model calculated a 51.5% probability that you might face challenges repaying, tipping the scales just over the threshold for denial as a bad credit risk. At the top of what influenced this decision was your checking account status showing less than 0 DM available, which stood out as a stronger warning sign compared to most approved applicants, where nearly half had no such account or better balances. Closely following that, your credit history of no credits taken or all paid previously raised similar concerns, as this is quite rare among those approved—only about 2% shared this profile, while the majority had a track record of timely payments elsewhere. On a more positive note, your real estate property ownership pushed back against the risk, acting as a stabilizing factor much like it does for around 30% of successful applicants. These three elements were the heaviest hitters in the model's thinking, with the first two driving toward caution and the third offering some reassurance. Your shorter requested duration of 12 months and lower amount also differed from typical approved loans averaging nearly 20 months and over 3200 DM, though they played supporting roles. While factors like your 4-7 years of employment, skilled job, and owning your housing aligned well with many good-credit profiles, they weren't enough to offset the key concerns. This prediction draws from how similar situations have played out historically, helping us make fair, data-driven choices without considering age or personal status. To move forward, we recommend building up your checking account balance, establishing a positive credit history with smaller credits, and reapplying once you've strengthened these areas—many applicants succeed after addressing such steps. You're close on the probability scale, so targeted improvements could make a real difference next time.
